briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 17, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Global Impacts
sections.content: Today, the top scientific panel and the National Academy of Sciences affirmed that climate change is fueling extreme weather events, with increasing evidence showing a link to disasters [28, 29, 30]. Indeed, Chicago and northern New York are experiencing smoke-filled skies from wildfire smoke as a 'new normal' [31, 38, 50], and the 2022 Italian glacier collapse was also found to be linked to climate change [35]. Climate change is having widespread impacts, causing weather-related disasters in agriculture [41], reshaping waterborne disease risks [44], affecting penguin species [40], and even contributing to sleep deprivation among populations in Southern India [54]. Furthermore, extreme weather has become the 'new standard' in the UK, with East London experiencing its third heatwave peak [55, 57], and a 'Super El Niño' is predicted, with warnings that 2027 could be the hottest year on record [56]. These examples clearly demonstrate that climate change is no longer a future threat, but a severe ongoing issue.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, local governments are actively pursuing carbon neutrality efforts. Mapo-gu is promoting the 'Re-planting 5 Million Trees' initiative [1, 2], and Yangpyeong-gun held working-level training and discussions to strengthen its capacity for implementing the carbon neutrality basic plan [3, 5]. Suncheon-si is operating the 'Carbon Moa Tantan-e' platform, encouraging citizens to participate in carbon neutrality and offering up to 200,000 KRW in rewards [4, 8, 10], thereby promoting everyday practices. Furthermore, Gwangyang Jungjin Elementary School's attached kindergarten won an excellence award in a short-form video contest for carbon neutrality practices [7], and education to learn about carbon neutrality through cultivating potatoes in home gardens is underway [6], demonstrating parallel efforts to raise awareness through education. Corporate and institutional collaborations are also continuing, with the Korea Environmental Industry & Technology Institute and the Korea Development Bank cooperating to achieve green growth and carbon neutrality [9], M&S Holdings is expanding its eco-friendly biomass business [11], and the Gurye-gun Federation of Korea Rural Leaders is practicing carbon neutrality by collecting pesticide bottles [12, 13], showing various entities contributing to achieving carbon neutrality.

sections.title: 🌱 Progress and Challenges in Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: In the renewable energy sector, significant progress is being reported both domestically and internationally. Good Energy has signed its largest-ever renewable energy supply contract with Ørsted [14], and SSE announced an 83% increase in Q1 network investment and expanded renewable energy generation [21], indicating continued large-scale investments. Domestically, the establishment of renewable energy and ESS (Energy Storage System) distribution in semiconductor cluster power grids is accelerating [16], and Korea East-West Power is enhancing grid acceptance of renewable energy using AI-based ESS [22, 27]. Furthermore, a legal framework for resident-participatory renewable energy projects is being established [18, 24], and a brokerage pilot project to vitalize direct power purchase agreements (PPAs) between renewable energy producers and companies is being implemented [20], highlighting efforts to activate the market and expand participation. However, the EDP CEO pointed out that EU member states have failed to implement renewable energy permits [17], emphasizing the importance of policy support. Sri Lanka is expanding investments in the renewable energy sector [15], and Australia is mandating AI data centers to bear their own renewable energy costs [26], showing that the global transition to renewable energy is an inevitable trend.

sections.title: 🌍 Social Discourse and International Cooperation for Climate Change Response
sections.content: Climate change is emerging as a core agenda item in global social and political discourse. Interestingly, political representatives appear to hold more polarized views on climate change than their constituents [32], suggesting complexity in the policymaking process. In developing countries like East Timor, responding to the climate crisis is recognized as a national task [37], and the perspective that cities are a crucial driving force in leading climate change response has also been presented [46]. At the community level, investment in physical and mental infrastructure to respond to climate change is being emphasized [34], and practical concerns, such as construction issues in fire-prone areas [49], are deepening. Internationally, the IFHA (International Federation of Horseracing Authorities) held its historic first Global Sustainability Summit, addressing climate change as a key agenda item [53], demonstrating active cooperation and discussions for climate action across various sectors. Research indicating that Brazil's high-altitude forests have been shaped by climate change and indigenous peoples over 6,000 years [45] provides a deeper understanding of current climate issues through the long-standing interaction between humanity and nature [47].

briefing.closing: As you have seen in today's briefing, climate change is having widespread global impacts, and efforts from all sectors of society and international discussions to address it are becoming increasingly crucial. This concludes today's climate briefing for July 17, 2026.
